config.title: Open-Air Cinema in Berlin - FAQ
config.subtitle: Quick answers on when Berlin's open-air cinema season runs, how to find a film in English, what it costs, and which Freiluftkino to pick.

items.q: When is open-air cinema season in Berlin?
items.a: Berlin's open-air cinemas (Freiluftkinos) run from early May to around the middle of September, with most venues opening in the second half of May. Screenings begin around dusk, so in June and July the start is late, usually between 21:00 and 21:30. A few venues run afternoon and early-evening screenings too, especially Freiluftkino Hasenheide, which programmes up to three films a day.

items.q: Can I watch a film in English at a Berlin open-air cinema?
items.a: Yes, several do. Look for the codes in the listings: OV (Originalversion) means the original language with no subtitles, which for most big releases is English; OmU means the original language with German subtitles, so the audio is still English; and OmeU means the original language with English subtitles. Avoid a plain German dub, which usually has no code. Freiluftkino Kreuzberg, Rehberge, Friedrichshain, Kino Central and Sommerkino Kulturforum all regularly show films in their original version. Check the code next to the date, because the same title can screen dubbed one night and in English the next.

items.q: Which are the best open-air cinemas in Berlin for tourists?
items.a: For a central, easy night, Kino Central in the Haus Schwarzenberg courtyard is two minutes from Hackescher Markt, and Sommerkino Kulturforum offers deckchairs and a skyline view near Potsdamer Platz. For the full park experience with English filters, Freiluftkino Friedrichshain (Volkspark Friedrichshain), Freiluftkino Rehberge (Volkspark Rehberge, Wedding, right by U6 Rehberge) and Freiluftkino Kreuzberg are the ones I would plan around. Freiluftkino Hasenheide in Neukölln is the most dependable because it plays rain or shine.

items.q: How much do open-air cinema tickets cost, and do I need to book?
items.a: Tickets are between about 8.50 and 12 euros depending on the venue. Popular titles and warm weekends do sell out, so it is worth buying online in advance when you can. Smaller nights often still sell tickets at the box office, which tends to open around 30 minutes before the film. If you are choosing on the day, arrive with light still in the sky to get a decent seat and a drink from the bar before it starts.

items.q: What happens at an open-air cinema in Berlin if it rains?
items.a: It depends on the venue. Freiluftkino Hasenheide famously plays come rain or shine, so it is the safest bet for an unsettled forecast. Several other cinemas cancel in heavy rain and post the decision on their website or social media that afternoon, so check before you travel if a storm is forecast. Light rain is usually fine with a jacket or poncho, and Berlin evenings cool down quickly once the sun is gone, so bring a layer regardless of the forecast.

items.q: Is there an open-air cinema near the city centre or the walking tour?
items.a: Yes. Kino Central sits in the courtyard of Haus Schwarzenberg on Rosenthaler Strasse, a two-minute walk from Hackescher Markt, which is where my free walking tour ends. That makes it easy to walk the historic centre in the afternoon, eat around the Hackesche Höfe, and finish the day with a film in a hidden Mitte backyard without getting back on a train. Sommerkino Kulturforum near Potsdamer Platz is the other genuinely central option.
